title: Butter vs. Olive Oil: Which Fat Is Actually Good for You?
summary:
The U.S. government just flipped the food pyramid upside down—literally! The new version says eat lots of butter, beef fat (called tallow), and olive oil. But wait—nutrition experts are saying, "Not so fast!"

Here's the deal: there are two main types of fat. Saturated fats (like butter and beef fat) are solid at room temperature. Unsaturated fats (like olive oil and seed oils) are liquid. For years, science has shown that swapping animal fats for plant-based ones is better for your heart and lowers your risk of getting sick.

"You get the biggest bang for your buck," says Dr. Deirdre Tobias, a nutrition scientist. She means that eating plant fats instead of animal fats gives you the most health benefit.

So why did the government change the guidelines? Usually, they listen to a special committee of scientists. But this time, they used a different group—and some of those people had connections to the meat and dairy industry. That's a bit suspicious, right?

Experts also worry that if people eat more meat and butter, they'll eat fewer fruits, veggies, and grains. That means less fiber—which your body needs to stay healthy.

The bottom line? Science says nuts, seeds, fish, and olive oil are your friends. Butter once in a while is okay, but don't make it your main fat. Your heart will thank you!
why_it_matters: This helps you decide what to eat for snacks and meals—choosing olive oil over butter can keep your heart healthy now and when you grow up.

keywords.explanation: Fats that are solid at room temperature, like butter and beef fat
Fats that are liquid at room temperature, like olive oil and seed oils
A plant-based fat that is liquid and good for your heart
A solid fat from animals, often used in cooking
The organ that pumps blood; eating healthy fats keeps it strong
A part of plants that helps digestion and keeps you healthy

Why did nutrition experts question the new guidelines?

options: They eat fewer fruits, vegetables, and grains

questions.question: What do you get less of if you eat fewer fruits and veggies?

background_read: For many years, doctors thought all fat was bad, but now we know that different fats affect our bodies in different ways.
Eating too much saturated fat can raise cholesterol and increase the risk of heart disease.
The U.S. government updates its dietary guidelines every five years based on the latest science.
Article_Structure: WHO: The U.S. government and nutrition experts
WHAT: The new food pyramid suggests eating more butter and beef fat, which experts think is unhealthy
WHERE: United States (government guidelines for the whole country)
WHY: Because the government used advisors with ties to the meat and dairy industry

perspectives.description: Some people say natural animal fats are fine in moderation and that the old guidelines were too strict.
Most nutrition scientists say the guidelines ignore decades of evidence and are influenced by the meat and dairy industry.
Both views agree that eating too much of any fat is unhealthy; balance is key.
